Her fierce form is not frightening to devotees — it is deeply reassuring.
Kali destroys all negativity — fear, ego, anger, and evil — to protect her children.
She is worshipped with great devotion in Bengal and many parts of India.
The Kalighat Temple in Kolkata is one of her most sacred shrines.
Kali Puja is celebrated during Diwali in Bengal with great devotion and ceremony.
Despite her fearsome appearance, Kali is seen as the most compassionate mother.
Her name means time — reminding us that time destroys all things, but love endures!

items.name: Lord Indra
items.text:
Lord Indra is the king of the gods (Devas) in Hindu mythology.
He is the god of thunder, lightning, storms, and rain.
Indra wields the Vajra — a divine thunderbolt weapon — as his signature weapon.
His vehicle is Airavata — a magnificent white elephant.
Indra lives in Swarga (heaven) and rules over all the heavenly realms.
He is celebrated for his victory over the demon Vritra — releasing the monsoon rains.
Indra's rain is vital for crops and life on Earth — making him deeply important to farmers.
Despite his great power, Indra sometimes acted out of pride and had to face consequences.
Krishna famously taught a lesson to Indra by stopping the worship of Indra and worshipping Govardhan hill instead.
Indra is a reminder that even the most powerful must remain humble!

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text:
Lord Rama is the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu and the hero of the Ramayana.
He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya.
Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam — the ideal man who always follows dharma.
He was a perfect son, husband, brother, and king.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.
During his exile, his wife Sita was kidnapped by the demon king Ravana of Lanka.
Rama, with his army of monkeys led by Hanuman, crossed the ocean and defeated Ravana.
His return to Ayodhya is celebrated every year as Diwali — the festival of lights.
Rama's story teaches us about truth, duty, love, and the victory of good over evil.
The words Jai Shri Ram are a greeting of devotion and deep respect across India!

items.name: Goddess Parvati
items.text:
Goddess Parvati is the divine mother in Hinduism and the consort of Lord Shiva.
She is the goddess of love, fertility, devotion, and divine strength.
Parvati is the gentle, beautiful form of the supreme mother goddess.
She is also known as Uma, Gauri, and Annapurna in different traditions.
Parvati performed intense tapas (meditation and austerity) to win the love of Lord Shiva.
Her determination and devotion are celebrated as the highest ideal of love and patience.
She is the mother of Lord Ganesha and Lord Murugan.
Annapurna, her form as the goddess of food, is worshipped to ensure the household never goes hungry.
Parvati teaches us that love combined with determination can achieve anything.
She is the embodiment of shakti — divine feminine power that sustains all of creation!

items.name: Diwali
items.text:
Diwali is the most popular festival in India and is called the Festival of Lights.
It is celebrated every year in October or November.
People light thousands of oil lamps and candles to fill their homes with light.
Fireworks light up the night sky with beautiful colours.
Diwali celebrates the return of Lord Rama to Ayodhya after 14 years of exile.
Goddess Lakshmi is worshipped during Diwali for wealth and good fortune.
Families exchange gifts and sweets with each other during this festival.
Homes are decorated with colourful rangoli patterns on the floor.
Children love Diwali for the sweets, new clothes, and sparklers!
Diwali is celebrated not just in India but by Hindus all over the world!

items.name: Holi
items.text:
Holi is the festival of colours, celebrated in spring across India.
People throw bright coloured powders and water at each other.
Everyone is covered in pink, red, green, yellow, and blue by the end!
Holi celebrates the victory of good over evil — the story of Prahlad and Holika.
On the night before Holi, bonfires are lit — this is called Holika Dahan.
Holi marks the beginning of spring and the end of winter.
People forget old fights and grudges and celebrate together joyfully.
Tasty sweets like gujiya and thandai are made and shared.
Holi teaches us that differences don't matter — everyone gets coloured the same!
Holi is celebrated with the same joy and laughter all across India!

items.name: Navratri
items.text:
Navratri means nine nights and is a festival celebrated for nine days.
It is dedicated to Goddess Durga and her nine forms.
Each day, a different form of Durga is worshipped with special colours and prayers.
Garba and Dandiya are traditional dances performed during Navratri.
Women and girls wear beautiful colourful dresses and dance in circles.
During these nine days, many devotees observe fasts and eat only simple food.
Navratri is celebrated in spring and autumn — twice a year!
The tenth day is Vijayadashami, also called Dussehra, celebrating Rama's victory over Ravana.
In Gujarat, the Navratri celebrations are especially grand and famous.
Navratri teaches devotion, discipline, and celebration of the divine feminine!

items.name: Raksha Bandhan
items.text:
Raksha Bandhan is a beautiful festival celebrating the bond between brothers and sisters.
The word means the bond of protection.
On this day, a sister ties a colourful thread called a rakhi on her brother's wrist.
The rakhi is a symbol of her love and prayer for his long, happy life.
In return, the brother promises to always protect his sister and gives her a gift.
Raksha Bandhan falls on a full moon day in the month of Shravan.
Even cousins, friends, and neighbours celebrate this special bond together.
Soldiers receive rakhis from women as a sign of protection and blessings.
Historically, queens sent rakhis to kings for protection — even across enemy lines!
Raksha Bandhan teaches love, care, and the sacred duty of family!

items.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
items.text:
Ganesh Chaturthi is one of the most joyous festivals in India.
It celebrates the birthday of Lord Ganesha — the elephant-headed god of wisdom.
Beautifully decorated clay idols of Ganesha are installed in homes and public spaces.
For ten days, devotees offer prayers, sweets (especially modak), and flowers.
Mumbai's Ganesh Chaturthi celebrations are among the grandest in the world.
On the final day, the idol is taken in a grand procession to a river, lake, or sea.
Devotees immerse the idol in water — this is called visarjan.
Loud music, dancing, and the chant of Ganpati Bappa Morya fill the streets!
The festival was made a public celebration by Lokmanya Bal Gangadhar Tilak in 1893.
Ganesh Chaturthi brings communities together in a spirit of unity and devotion!

items.name: Pongal
items.text:
Pongal is a harvest festival celebrated in Tamil Nadu in January.
The word Pongal means to boil or overflow — referring to the boiling of the harvest rice.
The festival lasts for four days — Bhogi, Thai Pongal, Mattu Pongal, and Kaanum Pongal.
Farmers thank the Sun, rain, and cattle for giving them a good harvest.
On the main day, fresh rice is cooked in a clay pot with jaggery, milk, and cashews.
When the rice boils over, everyone shouts Pongalo Pongal — a cry of joy and abundance!
Cows and bulls are decorated with flowers and worshipped on Mattu Pongal.
Kolam (rangoli) patterns made of rice flour decorate every doorstep.
Pongal is a festival of gratitude — thanking nature for its gifts.
It is one of the oldest festivals in India — mentioned in ancient Tamil literature!

items.name: Onam
items.text:
Onam is the harvest festival of Kerala and one of the most celebrated festivals in South India.
It falls in the Malayalam month of Chingam (August to September).
Onam celebrates the return of the mythical King Mahabali to visit his beloved people.
The most famous activity of Onam is the Pookalam — a beautiful flower carpet.
Vallamkali, the thrilling snake boat race on the backwaters, is watched by thousands.
The Onam Sadhya is a grand feast of 26 dishes served on a banana leaf.
People wear new white clothes with gold borders during Onam.
Mahabali was a great and just king — so beloved that Vishnu granted him the gift of visiting Kerala once a year.
Onam is a festival of prosperity, unity, and the eternal love between a king and his people.
The joy of Onam is captured in the saying — Maveliyude Naadu, Mannarum Naadum!

items.name: Ugadi
items.text:
Ugadi is the New Year festival celebrated in Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Karnataka.
The word Ugadi means the start of a new age in Sanskrit.
It falls on the first day of the Chaitra month — usually in March or April.
Ugadi marks the beginning of spring, new crops, and new beginnings.
The Panchanga (almanac) is read on this day to know the year's predictions.
Ugadi Pachadi is a special dish made with six tastes — sweet, sour, bitter, spicy, salty, and astringent.
Each taste represents one of the emotions of life — teaching us to accept all of life equally.
Homes are decorated with mango leaves tied across the door frame.
People wear new clothes, visit temples, and share sweets with family and friends.
Ugadi teaches us to welcome all of life's flavours with equal grace!

items.name: Janmashtami
items.text:
Janmashtami is the joyous celebration of the birth of Lord Krishna.
Krishna was born at midnight — so celebrations reach their peak at the stroke of twelve!
Temples are beautifully decorated and filled with devotional songs and dances.
The Dahi Handi is one of the most exciting Janmashtami traditions.
Young men form human pyramids to break a pot of curd hung high up — just like Krishna used to steal butter!
Devotees fast throughout the day and break their fast at midnight after the birth celebration.
Krishna's birthplace, Mathura and Vrindavan, sees some of the most spectacular celebrations.
The Ras Leela — a dance drama depicting Krishna's life — is performed in temples.
Children are dressed as little Krishnas with a flute, peacock feather, and yellow clothes.
Janmashtami reminds us that the divine chooses to be born among us — out of pure love!

items.name: Maha Shivaratri
items.text:
Maha Shivaratri is one of the most important festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva.
The name means the Great Night of Shiva — celebrated on the 14th night of the dark moon in Phalguna.
Devotees fast throughout the day and night and stay awake in prayer and devotion.
The Shivalingam is bathed with milk, honey, curd, and water — called Abhishekam.
Bilva leaves and white flowers are offered to Lord Shiva — deeply sacred to him.
It is believed that Shiva performed his cosmic Tandava dance on this night.
Visiting a Shiva temple on this night is considered especially auspicious.
The story of the poor hunter who accidentally worshipped Shiva all night and was liberated is a famous Shivaratri legend.
Devotees chant Om Namah Shivaya throughout the night.
Maha Shivaratri teaches that sincere devotion — even if accidental — is always accepted by Lord Shiva!

items.name: Eid
items.text:
Eid is one of the most important festivals in Islam, celebrated by Muslims worldwide.
There are two main Eid festivals — Eid ul-Fitr and Eid ul-Adha.
Eid ul-Fitr marks the end of Ramadan — the holy month of fasting.
After a month of fasting from dawn to sunset, Muslims celebrate with prayer, food, and gifts.
Eid ul-Adha honours the willingness of Prophet Ibrahim to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to God.
Both festivals begin with a special prayer called Namaz at the mosque.
New clothes are worn, sweets are shared, and families come together.
Sewaiyaan (vermicelli pudding) is a classic Eid sweet eaten across India and Pakistan.
Eid is a time of charity — giving to those in need is an important part of the celebration.
Eid teaches us gratitude, generosity, and the joy of sharing!

items.name: Christmas
items.text:
Christmas is celebrated on 25th December every year to mark the birth of Jesus Christ.
Christians believe Jesus was the Son of God, born to bring love and salvation to the world.
Christmas is celebrated with joy, lights, decorations, and the exchange of gifts.
The Christmas tree — decorated with lights and ornaments — is a beloved tradition worldwide.
Santa Claus is a beloved figure who is said to deliver gifts to children on Christmas Eve.
Churches hold midnight mass services on Christmas Eve — filled with carols and candlelight.
Christmas carols are special songs of joy and celebration sung during this season.
In India, Christmas is celebrated warmly by Christians and people of all faiths in cities like Goa, Mumbai, and Kolkata.
Christmas is a time of kindness, charity, and spending time with loved ones.
The spirit of Christmas is captured in the message: Peace on Earth, Goodwill to All!

items.name: Baisakhi
items.text:
Baisakhi is a harvest festival celebrated mainly in Punjab on April 13 or 14 every year.
It marks the beginning of the Sikh New Year and the harvest of the Rabi crop.
On this day in 1699, Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa — the community of baptised Sikhs.
Baisakhi is therefore one of the most sacred days in Sikhism.
Devotees visit the Gurdwara for prayers, hymns, and langar (community meal).
Bhangra and Giddha — energetic Punjabi folk dances — are performed with great joy.
Farmers celebrate the harvest by dancing and thanking God for a good season.
Baisakhi fairs are held across Punjab with food, music, and cultural events.
Amritsar's Golden Temple sees a massive gathering of Sikhs on Baisakhi.
Baisakhi reminds us that gratitude, courage, and community are the foundations of a good life!

items.name: Gudi Padwa
items.text:
Gudi Padwa is the New Year festival celebrated in Maharashtra, Goa, and parts of Karnataka.
It falls on the first day of the Hindu month of Chaitra — usually March or April.
A Gudi is a bright cloth tied to a bamboo pole, topped with a copper pot and neem leaves.
It is hoisted outside homes as a symbol of victory, prosperity, and the arrival of the New Year.
According to tradition, Lord Brahma created the universe on this day.
It is also said that Lord Rama returned to Ayodhya on this day after defeating Ravana.
People clean their homes, wear new clothes, and cook traditional sweets like puran poli.
Shrikhand and poori are classic Gudi Padwa treats enjoyed by families.
A procession called Shobha Yatra is taken out in cities with music and dance.
Gudi Padwa is a festival of new beginnings, hope, and the triumph of good!

items.name: Matsya Avatar
items.text:
Matsya is the first avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant fish.
The word Matsya means fish in Sanskrit.
In this form, Vishnu saved the first man, Manu, from a great flood.
He also rescued the sacred Vedas (ancient scriptures) that were stolen by a demon.
Matsya warned Manu to build a great boat and put all living creatures in it.
The tiny fish that Manu found in his hands kept growing bigger and bigger.
Matsya guided Manu's boat safely through the flood to dry land.
This avatar teaches us that God protects knowledge and life even in the worst disasters.
Matsya Avatar is one of the Dashavatara — ten great avatars of Vishnu.
The story shows that divine help always comes — sometimes in the most surprising forms!

items.name: Kurma Avatar
items.text:
Kurma is the second avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant tortoise.
Kurma means tortoise in Sanskrit.
The gods and demons once worked together to churn the cosmic ocean to get Amrita — the nectar of immortality.
They used Mount Mandara as a churning rod and the great serpent Vasuki as a rope.
But the mountain began to sink! Vishnu became Kurma and dived to the ocean bed.
He held Mount Mandara steady on his back so the churning could continue.
Many wonderful things emerged from the churning — Goddess Lakshmi, Dhanvantari, and finally Amrita.
Kurma teaches us the value of a strong foundation and steady, patient support.
Without Kurma's support, the whole effort would have failed.
This story is called Samudra Manthan — the Churning of the Cosmic Ocean!

items.name: Varaha Avatar
items.text:
Varaha is the third avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a mighty boar.
The demon Hiranyaksha had stolen the Earth (Bhudevi) and hidden her in the cosmic ocean.
Vishnu took the form of a massive boar and dived into the primordial waters.
After a fierce battle with the demon, Varaha lifted the Earth on his tusks.
He brought her back to her rightful place in the universe.
Varaha represents the power to recover and restore what is lost.
The Earth goddess Bhudevi is shown holding onto Varaha's tusk in many sculptures.
Beautiful Varaha sculptures can be seen in ancient temples across India.
This avatar teaches that Vishnu will go to any lengths to protect the Earth.
It is a reminder that the Earth is sacred and must always be protected!

items.name: Narasimha Avatar
items.text:
Narasimha is the fourth avatar of Lord Vishnu — half man, half lion.
Nara means man and Simha means lion in Sanskrit.
The demon king Hiranyakashipu had a boon that he could not be killed by man or animal.
His own son Prahlad was a devoted follower of Lord Vishnu.
Hiranyakashipu tried to kill Prahlad many times, but Vishnu always protected him.
At last, Vishnu appeared as Narasimha — not man, not animal.
He killed Hiranyakashipu at twilight, on a doorstep, on his lap — cleverly fulfilling every condition.
Narasimha teaches us that God always protects true devotees.
The story of Prahlad inspires children to be brave, devoted, and fearless.
Narasimha Jayanti is celebrated by devotees with fasting and prayer!

items.name: Vamana Avatar
items.text:
Vamana is the fifth avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a small dwarf Brahmin.
The demon king Bali was very powerful and had conquered the three worlds.
Bali was generous — he never refused anyone who came to him for a gift.
Vishnu took the form of a tiny Brahmin boy and went to Bali's court.
He asked for just three steps of land — as much as his small feet could cover.
Bali laughed and agreed.
But Vamana suddenly grew enormous — bigger than the entire universe!
With his first step he covered the Earth. With the second, the sky and heavens.
For the third step, Bali humbly bowed his own head — and was sent to the underworld.
Vishnu honoured Bali's devotion and generosity — granting him kingship of the underworld!

items.name: Parashurama Avatar
items.text:
Parashurama is the sixth avatar of Lord Vishnu — a fierce warrior-sage.
The word Parashu means axe — Parashurama means Rama with an axe.
He was born as a Brahmin but was a mighty warrior by the will of Vishnu.
Parashurama lived in an age when corrupt and arrogant kings were causing great suffering.
He is said to have rid the Earth of evil rulers twenty-one times.
He was the teacher of great warriors including Bhishma, Drona, and Karna.
Parashurama is one of the seven immortals (Chiranjeevis) — believed to still be alive!
He lives in deep meditation on the mountain Mahendragiri.
Kerala's land was given to Brahmins by Parashurama — this is a famous legend.
Parashurama represents righteous anger and the destruction of all injustice!

items.name: Rama Avatar
items.text:
Rama is the seventh and most celebrated avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He is the hero of the Ramayana — one of India's greatest epics.
Rama was born as the prince of Ayodhya, son of King Dasharatha.
He is called Maryada Purushottam — the ideal man who always follows dharma.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.
During exile, his wife Sita was kidnapped by the demon king Ravana of Lanka.
Rama, with his army of monkeys led by Hanuman, crossed the ocean and defeated Ravana.
His return to Ayodhya is celebrated every year as Diwali.
Rama's story teaches us about duty, honour, love, and the victory of good over evil.
Every Hindu knows the words Jai Shri Ram — a greeting of deep devotion and respect!

items.name: Krishna Avatar
items.text:
Krishna is the eighth and most beloved avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He appeared in an age of great war and confusion to restore dharma.
Krishna was born in a prison to save the world from the evil king Kansa.
He was secretly sent to Vrindavan where he grew up as a playful cowherd boy.
As a child Krishna killed many demons — showing divine power in a playful, loving form.
As a young man, he became a great king, statesman, and philosopher.
He delivered the Bhagavad Gita — the most important philosophical text in Hinduism.
Krishna's life — from playful child to wise teacher — is a complete guide to living well.
His birthday, Janmashtami, is one of the most joyously celebrated festivals in India.
Krishna's message endures: act with love, live with joy, and surrender to the divine!

items.name: Buddha Avatar
items.text:
The Buddha is considered the ninth avatar of Lord Vishnu in some Hindu traditions.
Siddhartha Gautama was born as a prince in Lumbini (modern-day Nepal) around 563 BCE.
He left his palace after seeing old age, sickness, and death for the first time.
After years of searching, he attained enlightenment while meditating under a Bodhi tree.
He became the Buddha — the Awakened One.
The Buddha taught the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path to end suffering.
His teaching is called Dharma — the path of righteousness and compassion.
Buddhism spread from India across Asia — to Sri Lanka, China, Japan, and beyond.
Ashoka, the great Indian emperor, converted to Buddhism and spread it worldwide.
The Buddha's message of compassion, non-violence, and mindfulness is more relevant today than ever!

items.name: Kalki Avatar
items.text:
Kalki is the tenth and final avatar of Lord Vishnu — but he has not yet appeared!
Hindu scriptures say Kalki will come at the end of the Kali Yuga — the current age of darkness.
He will ride a white horse, carry a blazing sword, and destroy all evil from the world.
After Kalki's arrival, a new golden age — the Satya Yuga — will begin.
The name Kalki means eternity, or one who destroys darkness.
Kalki is described as being born in a Brahmin family in a village called Shambhala.
He will defeat the forces of adharma (unrighteousness) in a final great battle.
The Kalki Purana describes his life and mission in detail.
Kalki reminds us that however dark things become, divine justice always prevails in the end.
The prophecy of Kalki gives hope — that a better world will come!

items.name: The Bhagavad Gita
items.text:
The Bhagavad Gita is one of the most important scriptures in Hinduism.
It is part of the great epic Mahabharata.
The Gita is a conversation between Prince Arjuna and his charioteer Lord Krishna.
The conversation takes place on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, just before a great war.
Arjuna was confused and sad — he did not want to fight his own family.
Krishna teaches Arjuna about duty, courage, and the nature of the soul.
The Gita has 18 chapters and 700 verses.
Krishna says: Do your duty without worrying about the result — this is the path of true wisdom.
The Gita teaches that the soul is eternal — it is never born and never dies.
Millions of people around the world read and follow the teachings of the Gita every day!

items.name: Karma Yoga
items.text:
Karma Yoga is one of the paths taught in the Bhagavad Gita.
Karma means action or deed.
Krishna teaches that every action we do has a consequence — good actions bring good results.
The key teaching is: do your duty well, without worrying about reward or result.
This is called Nishkama Karma — selfless action.
If you help someone only to get something in return, that is selfish.
But if you help because it is right, that is Karma Yoga.
Karma Yoga teaches us to be fully present and do our best in everything we do.
A student who studies hard without worrying about marks is practising Karma Yoga!
The world becomes a better place when people act with love and no selfishness.

items.name: Arjuna's Dilemma
items.text:
At the start of the Bhagavad Gita, Prince Arjuna faces a terrible problem.
He must fight in a war — but the enemies are his own cousins, uncles, and teachers.
Arjuna's hands tremble and he puts down his bow.
He tells Krishna: I cannot fight — it would be wrong to kill my own family.
Arjuna was a great warrior, but now he was confused and full of grief.
Krishna listens patiently and then begins to teach him the deepest wisdom.
Krishna says Arjuna must do his duty as a warrior — it is his dharma.
Running away from duty does not solve problems — it only creates more confusion.
Krishna helps Arjuna understand that the soul is immortal — no one truly dies.
Arjuna's story teaches us that it is okay to feel confused — wisdom comes when we seek the right guidance!

items.name: Gyaana Yoga
items.text:
Gyaana Yoga is the path of knowledge — one of the four paths in the Bhagavad Gita.
It means seeking deep understanding of the self and the universe.
Krishna teaches that the highest knowledge is knowing the soul (Atman) is part of God (Brahman).
We are not just the body — our true self is the eternal soul inside.
By studying, thinking deeply, and meditating, a person walks the path of Gyaana Yoga.
Knowledge removes ignorance, just like a lamp removes darkness.
When we understand the truth, we are no longer afraid of anything.
Adi Shankaracharya was a great example of someone who walked the path of Gyaana.
Gyaana Yoga is not just for scholars — it is for anyone who questions deeply and seeks truth.
Krishna says: of all gifts, the gift of knowledge is the greatest!

items.name: Bhakti Yoga
items.text:
Bhakti Yoga is the path of devotion — loving God with all your heart.
In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says Bhakti Yoga is the easiest and most direct path to God.
Bhakti means deep, pure love for the divine.
A devotee on this path prays, sings hymns, and thinks of God constantly.
Mirabai, Tukaram, and Kabir are famous saints who walked the path of Bhakti.
Bhakti does not need scholarship or wealth — it only needs a sincere, loving heart.
Singing bhajans and kirtans is a form of Bhakti Yoga accessible to everyone.
Krishna says: whoever offers me a leaf, a flower, water, or fruit with love — I accept it.
Bhakti Yoga teaches that the relationship between devotee and God is like that of a child and parent.
Through love and surrender, the heart finds peace and the devotee finds God!

items.name: The Eternal Soul
items.text:
One of the most important teachings in the Bhagavad Gita is about the soul.
Krishna tells Arjuna: the soul (Atman) can never be killed.
Weapons cannot cut it, fire cannot burn it, water cannot wet it, and wind cannot dry it.
The soul simply moves from one body to another — like changing old clothes for new ones.
This is the basis of the Hindu belief in reincarnation — rebirth after death.
The soul is eternal — it had no beginning and has no end.
What we call death is only the body dying — the soul continues its journey.
Krishna says: grieve not for those who die, for the soul is immortal.
The goal of life is to free the soul from the cycle of birth and death — this is moksha.
Understanding the eternal soul brings fearlessness, peace, and deep wisdom!

items.name: Nishkama Karma
items.text:
Nishkama Karma is one of the most powerful teachings of the Bhagavad Gita.
Nishkama means desireless — doing something without expecting a reward.
Krishna teaches: You have a right to perform your actions, but never to the fruits of those actions.
This means: do your best work, but do not be attached to whether you succeed or fail.
A farmer who plants seeds with love but accepts whatever harvest comes is practising Nishkama Karma.
A doctor who treats every patient with full dedication without thinking of payment practises this too.
Nishkama Karma frees us from anxiety, greed, and disappointment.
When we act without selfish desire, our actions become pure and powerful.
This teaching is relevant in school, work, family, and every form of service.
Gandhi was deeply inspired by Nishkama Karma — it guided his entire life of selfless service!

items.name: Raja Yoga
items.text:
Raja Yoga is the path of meditation and mental discipline in the Bhagavad Gita.
Raja means king — and Raja Yoga makes you the master (king) of your own mind.
Krishna teaches that the mind is our greatest friend — and also our greatest enemy.
A disciplined mind brings peace and clarity. An undisciplined mind brings suffering.
Raja Yoga involves sitting quietly, focusing the mind, and going inward.
The goal is to still all mental chatter and experience pure awareness.
Meditation — sitting in silence and observing your thoughts — is the core practice.
Patanjali's Yoga Sutras describe the eight steps (Ashtanga) of Raja Yoga in detail.
Swami Vivekananda introduced Raja Yoga to the Western world in the 1890s.
Through Raja Yoga, the mind becomes calm, focused, and full of inner light!

items.name: Dharma
items.text:
Dharma is one of the central concepts in the Bhagavad Gita and in all of Hindu thought.
The word dharma has many meanings — duty, righteousness, law, and the right way of living.
Krishna tells Arjuna: it is better to follow your own dharma imperfectly than another's dharma perfectly.
Every person has their own dharma — the right duty for their role in life.
A student's dharma is to study with sincerity and respect.
A parent's dharma is to love and guide their children.
A leader's dharma is to serve with fairness and protect those in their care.
When everyone follows their dharma, society is peaceful and prosperous.
When dharma is neglected, confusion and suffering arise.
Krishna says: whenever dharma declines, I will come again to restore it — this is his eternal promise!

items.name: The Battlefield Lesson
items.text:
The Bhagavad Gita begins on a battlefield — and this setting has a deep meaning.
Life itself is a kind of battlefield where we face difficult choices every day.
Just as Arjuna faced his own relatives as enemies, we sometimes face conflicts with those we love.
Krishna teaches that the right response is not to run away but to stand firm with wisdom.
The battlefield represents the struggle between our higher self and our lower desires.
Everyday we fight inner battles — between laziness and effort, fear and courage, selfishness and love.
Krishna's teaching is: fight your inner battles with wisdom, not anger.
The Gita says: a person who has conquered their own mind has conquered the whole world.
This is why the Gita is relevant not just in war — but in every moment of our lives.
The greatest battle is always the one within ourselves — and wisdom is the only true weapon!

items.name: The Pandavas
items.text:
The Pandavas are the five heroic brothers at the heart of the Mahabharata.
Their names are: Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva.
They were the sons of King Pandu of Hastinapura.
Yudhishthira was the eldest and known for his honesty and righteousness.
Bhima was the strongest of all — he could wrestle demons and elephants!
Arjuna was the greatest archer in the world — his skill was unmatched.
Nakula and Sahadeva were the twins, known for their wisdom and horse skills.
The Pandavas faced many hardships — exile, betrayal, and war — but never gave up.
Their mother Kunti prayed for them and Lord Krishna guided and protected them.
The Pandavas' story teaches us about brotherhood, courage, and the victory of dharma!

items.name: The Kauravas
items.text:
The Kauravas are the one hundred sons of King Dhritarashtra of Hastinapura.
Duryodhana was the eldest and most powerful of the Kauravas.
The Kauravas were the rivals and cousins of the Pandavas.
Duryodhana was proud, jealous, and deeply resentful of the Pandavas' virtues.
He refused to give even five villages to the Pandavas — leading to the great war.
Shakuni, their maternal uncle, advised and guided their scheming against the Pandavas.
Despite having a hundred brothers and a powerful army, the Kauravas were defeated.
Their downfall was caused by pride, jealousy, and blind love from their father Dhritarashtra.
The Kauravas are a reminder that power and numbers mean nothing without dharma.
Evil, no matter how powerful, cannot defeat righteousness in the end!

items.name: Battle of Kurukshetra
items.text:
The Battle of Kurukshetra is the great war at the centre of the Mahabharata.
It was fought between the Pandavas and the Kauravas — two branches of the same family.
The battle lasted for 18 days on the field of Kurukshetra in Haryana, India.
Lord Krishna served as the charioteer of Arjuna and guided him with wisdom.
It was before this battle that Krishna shared the wisdom of the Bhagavad Gita.
Millions of soldiers, elephants, horses, and chariots were part of this vast war.
Great warriors like Bhishma, Drona, Karna, and Abhimanyu fought on the battlefield.
In the end, the Pandavas won — but the cost was enormous. Many heroes fell.
The battle teaches that war is the last option and that dharma must always be upheld.
Kurukshetra is still a holy place today — visited by millions of pilgrims!

items.name: Draupadi
items.text:
Draupadi is one of the most powerful women in the Mahabharata.
She was born from a sacred fire during a yagna performed by her father, King Drupada.
Draupadi was the wife of all five Pandava brothers.
She was known for her beauty, intelligence, fierce spirit, and deep devotion to Krishna.
In one of the darkest moments of the story, she was publicly humiliated by the Kauravas.
She prayed to Krishna with all her heart, and he miraculously protected her honour.
Draupadi never forgot the injustice — her fire inspired the Pandavas to fight for dharma.
She asked only one thing after the war: justice and respect for all women.
Draupadi teaches us that a woman's dignity must always be honoured and protected.
She is a symbol of resilience, devotion, and the power of righteous anger!

items.name: Karna
items.text:
Karna is one of the most tragic and admired heroes of the Mahabharata.
He was actually the eldest son of Kunti — making him the Pandavas' own brother!
But Kunti gave him up as a baby, and he was raised by a charioteer named Adhiratha.
Because of his humble birth, Karna was often mocked despite his great talent.
He was the greatest rival of Arjuna in archery — their skill was equal.
Karna was known for his incredible generosity — he never refused anyone who asked for help.
He was a loyal friend to Duryodhana and stayed by his side till the end.
Krishna revealed the truth of his birth to Karna — but Karna chose loyalty over family.
Karna was finally defeated by Arjuna at Kurukshetra.
His life teaches us about loyalty, generosity, dignity, and the tragedy of choosing the wrong side!

items.name: Bhishma
items.text:
Bhishma is one of the greatest and most tragic figures of the Mahabharata.
His original name was Devavrata — son of King Shantanu and the goddess Ganga.
He is called Bhishma because of his terrible vow: he swore never to marry or become king.
This was to allow his father to marry Satyavati, a fisherman's daughter.
Bhishma had the boon of choosing his own time of death — he could not die without his will.
He was the grandsire of both the Pandavas and Kauravas and was loved by all.
In the war, Bhishma was bound by oath to fight for the Kauravas even though he loved the Pandavas.
He fell on the tenth day of battle, pierced by Arjuna's arrows.
He lay on a bed of arrows for 58 days, waiting for the auspicious moment to leave his body.
Bhishma's life teaches us about the terrible cost of rigid duty without wisdom!

items.name: Abhimanyu
items.text:
Abhimanyu is one of the most heroic young warriors in the Mahabharata.
He was the son of the great archer Arjuna and Subhadra, Krishna's sister.
Abhimanyu learned the art of entering the Chakravyuha (a circular military formation) while in the womb!
He heard his father Arjuna explaining it to his mother — but Subhadra fell asleep before Arjuna explained how to exit.
So Abhimanyu knew how to enter the Chakravyuha but not how to come out.
On the thirteenth day of the Kurukshetra war, Abhimanyu bravely entered alone.
He fought off many great warriors single-handedly with breathtaking skill.
But trapped inside and surrounded, he was unfairly killed by multiple warriors at once.
Abhimanyu died at just 16 years old — a symbol of courage, brilliance, and sacrifice.
His story reminds us that bravery and brilliance shine even in the most hopeless of circumstances!

items.name: Vidura
items.text:
Vidura is one of the wisest and most virtuous characters of the Mahabharata.
He was the half-brother of Dhritarashtra and Pandu — born of a servant woman.
Despite his lower birth, Vidura became the chief minister of Hastinapura.
He was known as the embodiment of dharma — always speaking the truth.
Vidura repeatedly warned King Dhritarashtra against the wicked schemes of Duryodhana.
He pleaded with the king to give the Pandavas their rightful share — but was ignored.
When the Pandavas were in danger, Vidura secretly helped and protected them.
His wisdom is recorded in Vidura Niti — a collection of maxims on good governance and ethics.
Vidura Niti says: a true friend tells you the truth even when it is painful to hear.
Vidura teaches us that speaking truth and standing by the righteous is the highest duty!

items.name: Shakuni
items.text:
Shakuni was the maternal uncle of the Kauravas and one of the most cunning characters in the Mahabharata.
He was the prince of Gandhara (present-day Afghanistan and Pakistan).
Shakuni carried a deep grudge against the Kuru dynasty for the suffering of his family.
He used his cunning and mastery of dice to manipulate events against the Pandavas.
It was Shakuni who rigged the famous game of dice where Yudhishthira lost everything — including Draupadi.
Shakuni always worked from the shadows — advising Duryodhana and fuelling his hatred.
He was brilliant but used his brilliance entirely for destruction and revenge.
Shakuni was finally killed by Sahadeva on the 18th day of the Kurukshetra war.
His story teaches us that intelligence used for revenge and manipulation leads only to ruin.
Shakuni is a reminder that even great cleverness without righteousness destroys both the user and the used!

items.name: Drona
items.text:
Drona, also known as Dronacharya, was the greatest teacher of warfare in the Mahabharata.
He taught both the Pandavas and Kauravas the art of archery and military strategy.
Drona's favourite student was Arjuna — he believed Arjuna would become the greatest archer in the world.
The story of Ekalavya is one of the most famous and moving stories involving Drona.
Ekalavya, a tribal boy, taught himself archery by worshipping a clay statue of Drona.
He became so skilled that Drona asked for his right thumb as Guru Dakshina (teacher's fee).
Ekalavya gave it without hesitation — showing supreme devotion to his guru.
In the war, Drona fought on the side of the Kauravas — bound by duty to Hastinapura.
He was eventually defeated through a trick involving false news of his son Ashwatthama's death.
Drona's story raises deep questions about duty, loyalty, and the ethics of a teacher!

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text:
Lord Rama is the hero of the Ramayana and the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu.
He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya.
Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam — the ideal man who always followed dharma.
He was a perfect son, husband, brother, and king.
Rama was exiled to the forest for 14 years due to a promise his father made.
During his exile, his wife Sita was kidnapped by the demon king Ravana.
Rama bravely went to Lanka with an army of monkeys to rescue Sita.
With the help of Hanuman and Sugriva, he defeated Ravana and saved Sita.
Rama's return to Ayodhya is celebrated every year as Diwali.
Rama's story teaches us that a life of truth, duty, and love is the greatest life!

items.name: Sita
items.text:
Sita is the beloved wife of Lord Rama and the heroine of the Ramayana.
She was found as a baby in a furrow of the earth by King Janaka — hence called Janaki.
Sita is worshipped as an ideal of purity, love, courage, and devotion.
She chose to follow Rama into the forest during his 14 years of exile out of pure love.
When Ravana kidnapped her, Sita never gave up hope — she kept her faith in Rama.
In Lanka, Sita refused all of Ravana's riches and stayed loyal to Rama.
She is considered a form of Goddess Lakshmi.
Sita passed through fire (Agni Pariksha) to prove her purity — and the fire did not burn her.
The story of Sita teaches us about dignity, courage, and unbreakable devotion.
Sita's gentle but fierce spirit makes her one of the greatest heroines in all of literature!

items.name: Ravana
items.text:
Ravana was the powerful king of Lanka and the main villain of the Ramayana.
He had ten heads and twenty arms, symbolising his vast knowledge and power.
Ravana was actually a great scholar — he knew the Vedas and played the veena beautifully.
He was a devoted worshipper of Lord Shiva and had received many powerful boons.
But Ravana's pride and ego led him to kidnap Sita — and this became his downfall.
Despite being intelligent and powerful, Ravana chose wrong over right.
Rama finally killed Ravana after a fierce battle, ending his terror.
Ravana's story teaches us that knowledge and power without humility can destroy us.
Dussehra is celebrated by burning effigies of Ravana to mark the victory of good over evil.
In some traditions, Ravana is also remembered as a great Shiva devotee and learned king!

items.name: Hanuman's Leap to Lanka
items.text:
One of the greatest moments in the Ramayana is when Hanuman leaps across the ocean to Lanka.
Sita had been kidnapped by Ravana and Rama needed to know where she was.
Hanuman volunteered to search for Sita.
He climbed a mountain, took a deep breath, and leaped across the vast ocean!
On the way, he faced sea monsters and storms — but nothing stopped him.
In Lanka, Hanuman searched carefully and finally found Sita in the Ashoka garden.
He spoke to her gently and gave her Rama's ring as proof he was Rama's messenger.
Before leaving, Hanuman caused havoc in Lanka — destroying Ravana's palace and garden.
Ravana's soldiers set his tail on fire — but Hanuman used the fire to burn Lanka!
Hanuman's leap is a story of devotion, courage, and love that knows no limit!

items.name: Lakshmana
items.text:
Lakshmana is the devoted younger brother and constant companion of Lord Rama.
When Rama was sent into exile, Lakshmana refused to stay behind.
He chose to leave the comfort of the palace and follow Rama into the forest out of pure love.
Lakshmana built huts, gathered food, and stood guard every night to protect Rama and Sita.
He barely slept during the entire 14 years of exile — his protectiveness was fierce.
He drew the famous Lakshman Rekha to protect Sita — a line she must not cross.
In the war against Ravana, Lakshmana killed many great warriors including Indrajit.
He was once severely wounded in battle — Hanuman flew to the Himalayas to bring the healing herb.
Lakshmana is worshipped as a symbol of brotherly love, sacrifice, and selfless devotion.
His relationship with Rama is considered the ideal of brotherhood in all of Hindu tradition!

items.name: Vibhishana
items.text:
Vibhishana was the younger brother of Ravana — but unlike his brother, he was righteous and good.
He repeatedly advised Ravana to return Sita and avoid war with Rama.
But Ravana, blinded by pride, refused to listen.
Vibhishana finally left Lanka and surrendered to Rama — asking for his protection.
Some advisors said Rama should not trust an enemy's brother.
But Rama said: whoever comes to me seeking refuge — I will protect them always.
Vibhishana gave Rama vital information about Lanka's defences and Ravana's weakness.
After Ravana's defeat, Rama crowned Vibhishana as the king of Lanka.
Vibhishana is celebrated as a devotee who chose dharma over family loyalty.
His story teaches us that truth and righteousness are higher duties than blind family loyalty!

items.name: Ayodhya
items.text:
Ayodhya is the sacred birthplace of Lord Rama and one of the holiest cities in Hinduism.
The name Ayodhya means a city that cannot be conquered — a city of peace.
It is located on the banks of the Sarayu river in Uttar Pradesh, India.
Ayodhya is one of the seven most sacred pilgrimage cities in Hinduism.
The magnificent Ram Mandir was inaugurated in 2024 on the site believed to be Rama's birthplace.
Millions of devotees visit Ayodhya every year to seek the blessings of Lord Rama.
The city is filled with temples, ghats (river steps), and the atmosphere of deep devotion.
Deepavali (Diwali) is celebrated with exceptional grandeur in Ayodhya.
The Sarayu river is considered sacred — pilgrims bathe in its waters for purification.
Ayodhya is not just a city — it is the heartbeat of Hindu civilisation and devotion!

items.name: Jatayu
items.text:
Jatayu is one of the most moving and heroic characters in the Ramayana.
He was a mighty eagle — the king of vultures — and an old friend of King Dasharatha.
When Ravana was flying back to Lanka with the kidnapped Sita, Jatayu spotted them.
Despite being old and knowing he faced the most powerful demon king, Jatayu attacked Ravana without hesitation.
He fought bravely in the skies to stop Ravana and save Sita.
Ravana cut off Jatayu's wings with his sword — but the great eagle refused to give up.
Jatayu held on to life long enough to tell Rama what had happened and where Sita was taken.
When Rama found Jatayu dying, he wept and held the great bird like a father.
Rama performed Jatayu's last rites himself — the highest honour.
Jatayu teaches us that true heroism is not about winning — it is about acting with courage for what is right!

items.name: Sugriva
items.text:
Sugriva is the monkey king who became one of Rama's most important allies in the Ramayana.
He was the younger brother of the mighty Vali, who had unfairly driven him away from his kingdom.
Sugriva and Rama made a pact — Rama would help him defeat Vali, and Sugriva would help find Sita.
After Vali's defeat, Sugriva became the king of Kishkindha.
He organised his vast monkey army to search for Sita across the entire world.
Hanuman, his greatest general, was sent to Lanka and found Sita there.
Sugriva then led his army to help Rama build the bridge to Lanka and fight Ravana.
Sugriva is an example of loyalty and keeping one's promises.
His kingdom of Kishkindha was believed to be located in present-day Karnataka.
Sugriva's alliance with Rama shows that even the humblest help can change the course of history!

items.name: The Ram Setu
items.text:
The Ram Setu, or Adam's Bridge, is a chain of limestone shoals connecting India and Sri Lanka.
According to the Ramayana, it was built by Rama's army of monkeys to cross over to Lanka.
The bridge is called Nala Setu after the engineer monkey Nala, son of Vishwakarma, who designed it.
The monkeys brought huge boulders and stones that floated because the name of Rama was written on them.
The devotion of Rama's army made even stones float — a miracle of faith and love.
The bridge stretches for about 50 km across the Palk Strait.
Satellite images taken by NASA show a line of shoals that match the description of the Ram Setu.
Rameshwaram in Tamil Nadu at one end and Mannar Island in Sri Lanka at the other.
The Ram Setu is one of the most discussed and debated ancient structures in the world.
Whether seen as a geological formation or a divine miracle, the Ram Setu is a symbol of extraordinary faith!

items.name: Valmiki
items.text:
Valmiki is one of the greatest sages in Hindu tradition.
He is known as the Adi Kavi — the first poet in Sanskrit literature.
Valmiki composed the Ramayana — one of the longest and most beloved epics in the world.
Interestingly, Valmiki was once a highway robber called Ratnakar.
A meeting with the sage Narada changed his life — he meditated deeply and was reborn as a great saint.
He meditated so long that ant hills (valmika) grew around him — giving him his name.
When Sita was exiled, she found refuge in Valmiki's ashram.
Valmiki taught her children, Lava and Kusa, to sing the Ramayana.
His Ramayana has 24,000 verses spread across 7 chapters (Kandas).
Valmiki's life teaches us that anyone can change for the better, no matter their past!

items.name: Adi Shankaracharya
items.text:
Adi Shankaracharya was one of the greatest philosophers and saints of India.
He was born in Kaladi, Kerala, around 788 CE.
By the age of 8, he had mastered all the Vedas and ancient scriptures!
Shankaracharya walked the entire length of India on foot, spreading the message of Advaita Vedanta.
Advaita means non-duality — the teaching that God, the world, and the self are ultimately one.
He debated with great scholars and won, reviving Hindu philosophy at a time when it was declining.
He established four great mathas at the four corners of India: Badrinath, Dwarka, Puri, and Sringeri.
Shankara wrote beautiful hymns in praise of various deities — full of devotion and depth.
He attained Samadhi (spiritual liberation) at the young age of 32.
Shankaracharya is considered an incarnation of Lord Shiva himself by many devotees!

items.name: Mirabai
items.text:
Mirabai was a great saint-poetess from Rajasthan who lived in the 1500s.
From childhood, she was completely devoted to Lord Krishna — she saw him as her true husband.
She was a princess who married a Rajput king, but her heart always belonged to Krishna.
Her in-laws tried to stop her devotion and even tried to harm her — but she was miraculously protected.
Mirabai composed hundreds of beautiful bhajans (devotional songs) to Krishna.
She sang and danced in temples without caring what society thought of her.
Her bhajans are still sung all over India with great love and emotion.
She is said to have finally walked into a Krishna temple and merged with his idol.
Mirabai teaches us that true love for God is greater than all worldly power and status.
Her life is proof that devotion burns brighter than any obstacle!

items.name: Kabir
items.text:
Kabir was a great poet-saint of the 15th century whose teachings touched both Hindus and Muslims.
He was born in Varanasi and raised by a Muslim weaver family.
Kabir believed that God is one — whether you call him Rama or Allah.
His poems (dohas) were simple, direct, and full of wisdom.
He criticised blind rituals, caste discrimination, and religious hypocrisy fearlessly.
Kabir's dohas are memorised and sung across India to this day.
His most famous line: Dukh mein sumiran sab karein — in sorrow everyone remembers God.
Kabir never wrote his poems himself — they were passed on orally for generations.
His disciples — both Hindu and Muslim — continued his legacy after his death.
Kabir's message of love, equality, and inner devotion is as fresh today as it was 600 years ago!

items.name: Swami Vivekananda
items.text:
Swami Vivekananda was one of the greatest spiritual leaders and thinkers of modern India.
He was born Narendranath Datta in Kolkata in 1863.
He became the most famous disciple of the great mystic Sri Ramakrishna Paramahamsa.
Vivekananda introduced Hinduism to the Western world at the Parliament of World Religions in Chicago in 1893.
His speech beginning Sisters and Brothers of America electrified the audience!
He founded the Ramakrishna Mission — dedicated to education, health, and service.
Vivekananda believed that service to the poor is service to God.
His famous quote: Arise, awake, and stop not till the goal is reached is an inspiration to millions.
He wrote and spoke about Karma Yoga, Bhakti Yoga, Jnana Yoga, and Raja Yoga.
Swami Vivekananda's birthday (January 12) is celebrated as National Youth Day in India!

items.name: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa
items.text:
Ramakrishna Paramahamsa was one of the greatest mystics and saints of 19th-century India.
He was born in 1836 in a village in West Bengal.
From childhood he was deeply drawn to the divine — he would go into trances of ecstasy.
He served as a priest at the Dakshineswar Kali Temple near Kolkata.
Ramakrishna had profound mystical experiences with Goddess Kali — he saw her as a living reality.
He practised many different spiritual paths — Hindu, Islamic, and Christian — and had direct experiences in each.
He concluded that all religions lead to the same God — a message of universal harmony.
His most famous disciple was Narendranath — who became Swami Vivekananda.
Ramakrishna's teachings were simple: God can be seen; the Divine Mother is real and alive.
His life is a living example that complete surrender to God brings direct experience of the Divine!

items.name: Tukaram
items.text:
Tukaram was a great Bhakti saint from Maharashtra who lived in the 17th century.
He was a devotee of Lord Vitthal (Vishnu) at Pandharpur.
Tukaram was a simple farmer who experienced great personal tragedy — losing his first wife and son in a famine.
Through his grief, he turned completely to God and began composing abhangas — devotional poems.
His abhangas are written in simple Marathi and speak of love, surrender, and devotion to Vitthal.
Over 4,500 of his abhangas survive and are sung to this day.
Tukaram faced opposition from scholars who destroyed his handwritten manuscripts.
But according to tradition, Vithoba himself returned the manuscripts — recognising the saint's devotion.
The Warkari tradition kept his songs alive through oral tradition for centuries.
Tukaram is considered the greatest of the Maharashtra saints — his legacy lives in every bhajan sung!

items.name: Ramanujacharya
items.text:
Ramanujacharya was a great Vaishnava saint and philosopher of the 11th to 12th century.
He was born in Sriperumbudur near Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
Ramanuja taught Vishishtadvaita — the philosophy that the soul and God are distinct but always connected.
He believed deeply in devotion (bhakti) to Lord Vishnu as the path to liberation.
Ramanuja opened the doors of temples to people of all backgrounds — a revolutionary act of equality.
He walked across India spreading his teachings and inspiring thousands.
Ramanuja wrote commentaries on the Vedanta Sutras, Upanishads, and the Bhagavad Gita.
He trained 74 disciples to spread his teachings far and wide.
Ramanuja is honoured as one of the greatest saints in the Sri Vaishnava tradition.
His temple is in Srirangam — one of the largest temple complexes in the world!

items.name: Narada Muni
items.text:
Narada Muni is one of the most fascinating and important sages in Hindu mythology.
He is the son of Lord Brahma and travels freely between the three worlds — earth, heaven, and the underworld.
Narada is always depicted with a veena (string instrument), chanting the name of Narayana (Vishnu).
He is the divine messenger — carrying news between gods, sages, and humans.
Narada is the author of the Narada Bhakti Sutras — a beautiful text on the nature of devotion.
He played a key role in many stories — it was Narada who changed the life of the robber Ratnakar, who became Valmiki!
Narada also inspired Prahlad's devotion to Vishnu by teaching him in the womb.
Sometimes Narada is shown as a mischievous stirrer of events — but always in service of the divine plan.
He is one of the seven immortals (Chiranjeevis) — believed to still travel the universe.
Narada reminds us that devotion, music, and the name of God are the highest forms of worship!

items.name: Vyasa
items.text:
Vyasa, also known as Veda Vyasa, is one of the most important sages in all of Hindu tradition.
He is credited with compiling and organising the four Vedas — the oldest scriptures in Hinduism.
Vyasa also composed the Mahabharata — the longest epic poem ever written, with over 100,000 verses!
He is the grandfather figure of the Mahabharata — grandfather to both the Pandavas and Kauravas.
Vyasa also composed the 18 major Puranas — encyclopaedias of Hindu mythology and wisdom.
He dictated the Mahabharata to Lord Ganesha who wrote it down — Ganesha broke his own tusk as a pen!
Vyasa is one of the seven immortals (Chiranjeevis) — believed to still be alive in meditation.
Guru Purnima, the festival honouring all teachers, is celebrated on the birthday of Vyasa.
His full name — Krishna Dwaipayana Vyasa — reflects his dark complexion and island birth.
Vyasa's gift to humanity is immeasurable — he preserved the entire wisdom of ancient India!

comprehension.subject: Honeybees
comprehension.text: Honeybees are amazing little insects that live together in large groups called colonies. A colony can have up to 60,000 bees! Every bee in the colony has a job to do. The queen bee lays all the eggs. Worker bees collect nectar from flowers and bring it back to the hive. They turn the nectar into honey by fanning it with their wings to dry it out. Bees also help flowers grow by carrying pollen from one flower to another — this is called pollination. Without bees, many of the fruits and vegetables we eat would not exist. So the next time you see a bee buzzing around a flower, remember — it is doing very important work!

options: By fanning it with their wings
questions.correct: By fanning it with their wings

comprehension.subject: The Moon
comprehension.text: The Moon is Earth's closest neighbour in space. It is a big ball of rock that travels around the Earth. The Moon does not make its own light — it reflects light from the Sun, which is why it glows at night. The Moon is much smaller than the Earth — you could fit about 50 Moons inside the Earth! As the Moon travels around us, we see different shapes of it — these are called phases. Sometimes we see a full circle called a full moon. Sometimes we see just a thin curved shape called a crescent. There is no air or water on the Moon, so nothing can live there. In 1969, astronaut Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon. He said it felt like walking on soft sand!

questions.question: Where does the Moon get its light from?
options: It reflects light from the Sun
questions.correct: It reflects light from the Sun

Who was the first human to walk on the Moon?
What is a crescent?
options: A thin curved shape of the Moon
A Moon phase seen only in winter
The dark side of the Moon
